children.name: Picathartes
children.description: A genus of two unusual, medium-sized passerines endemic to the rainforests of West and Central Africa, commonly known as rockfowl and once considered to be related to crows or starlings. They are named for their habit of nesting colonially on cave walls and rock faces, building mud nests in the manner of swallows. Their striking bare heads, long tail, and upright stance give them a prehistoric appearance.

children.name: Picathartes gymnocephalus
children.description: A striking bird with a brightly coloured bald head in yellow and black, a white throat and neck, and a long tail, found in the rainforests of West Africa from Guinea to Ghana. It is a colonial nester, building mud cup nests on the roofs of caves and large rock overhangs. It forages on the forest floor for insects, snails, and small vertebrates, and is considered globally threatened due to habitat loss.

children.name: Picathartes oreas
children.description: A secretive, long-tailed bird with a brightly patterned bare head in blue, red, and black, and a grey neck, found in the rainforests of Central Africa from Cameroon to Gabon. It breeds colonially on cave walls and rock faces, building mud nests that are reused year after year. It is a shy, terrestrial forager that hops through the forest understorey, flipping over leaf litter in search of prey.
